coredata.dc:description: A Schiff base namely, 2-[(furan-3ylmethylene)-amino]-benzenethiol (2-FAB) was synthesized and its influence on the inhibition of corrosion in carbon steel immersed in 0.5 M H2SO4 was investigated by weight loss analysis, potentiodynamic polarization studies, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), scanning electron microscopy (SEM), atomic force microscopy (AFM) and FT-IR spectroscopy. The weight loss measurements showed that 2-FAB has an excellent inhibiting efficiency of 95.51% at a concentration of 250 ppm. The inhibitor efficiency was found to depend on both the concentration and molecular structure of the inhibitor. Potentiodynamic polarization curves revealed that the studied inhibitors represent a mixed-type, predominantly cathodic control. An equivalent circuit is suggested based on an analysis of EIS data. Surface analysis using scanning electron microscope (SEM) and atomic force microscopy (AFM) show a significant morphological improvement on the mild steel surface with the addition of 2-FAB. The negative value of standard free energy of adsorption in the presence of inhibitor suggests the spontaneous adsorption of inhibitors on the carbon steel surface. The Temkin and Langmuir adsorption isotherms were found to provide a accurate description of the adsorption behavior of the inhibitor. Taken as a whole, this work demonstrates that 2-FAB shows promising results in resisting the deterioration of carbon steel in 0.5 M H2SO4 environment. The inhibitor forms a protective film on the surface of the carbon steel, significantly reducing its corrosion rate.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A Schiff base namely, 2-[(furan-3ylmethylene)-amino]-benzenethiol (2-FAB) was synthesized and its influence on the inhibition of corrosion in carbon steel immersed in 0.5 M H2SO4 was investigated by weight loss analysis, potentiodynamic polarization studies, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), scanning electron microscopy (SEM), atomic force microscopy (AFM) and FT-IR spectroscopy. The weight loss measurements showed that 2-FAB has an excellent inhibiting efficiency of 95.51% at a concentration of 250 ppm. The inhibitor efficiency was found to depend on both the concentration and molecular structure of the inhibitor. Potentiodynamic polarization curves revealed that the studied inhibitors represent a mixed-type, predominantly cathodic control. An equivalent circuit is suggested based on an analysis of EIS data. Surface analysis using scanning electron microscope (SEM) and atomic force microscopy (AFM) show a significant morphological improvement on the mild steel surface with the addition of 2-FAB. The negative value of standard free energy of adsorption in the presence of inhibitor suggests the spontaneous adsorption of inhibitors on the carbon steel surface. The Temkin and Langmuir adsorption isotherms were found to provide a accurate description of the adsorption behavior of the inhibitor. Taken as a whole, this work demonstrates that 2-FAB shows promising results in resisting the deterioration of carbon steel in 0.5 M H2SO4 environment. The inhibitor forms a protective film on the surface of the carbon steel, significantly reducing its corrosion rate.

ce:keyword.ce:text: Temkin's adsorption isotherm and SEM & AFM

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Generally, corrosion can be understood as athe process of electrochemical reaction between a substance and its surrounding environment typically involving a metal. The deterioration of materials and their properties is often associated with this process (Fayomi and Popoola, 2019). Carbon steel is a popular material for building, construction and various mechanical applications due to its affordability and acceptable mechanical properties. It is frequently used in activities such as erecting boilers, industrial cleaning, oil-well acidizing, acid descaling, acid pickling, and petrochemical processes. However, one of its significant drawbacks is its susceptibility to corrosion. Acid solutions can cause significant damage to metallic materials; however, the use of corrosion inhibitors is prevalent in industries to mitigate or prevent such corrosion rates (Mandal et al., 2023a; Singh et al., 2023; Saha et al., 2022; Zeng et al., 2022). Although there have been ongoing advancements in the development of corrosion-resistant materials, chemical inhibitors are still frequently utilized in such materials, and currently, the most favored inhibitor is sulfuric acid.

Numerous organic compounds that contain polar functionalities in their molecules have the ability to inhibit the corrosion of mild steel in H2SO4 solution (Sengupta et al., 2021; Echihi et al., 2021; Obot and Obi-Egbedi, 2010). One widely accepted fact is that corrosion of carbon steel can be most effectively inhibited by organic inhibitors that incorporate hetero atoms like oxygen, nitrogen, or sulfur. Organic inhibitors can be used to prevent corrosion when carbon steel is exposed to an acidic environment (Ghareba and Omanovic, 2010; Musa et al., 2010; Boucherit et al., 2019a).

Abuthahir et al. studied about the mild steel immersed in 1-(8-hydroxyquinolin-2-yl-methyl) thiourea, using an aqueous solution that contained 60 parts per million of chlorine as a corrosion inhibitor (Rasheeda et al., 2022; Rezaeivala et al., 2022). MA Bedair and colleagues reported the use of a Schiff base compound based on benzidine for inhibiting corrosion of carbon steel in 1.0 M HCl (Mandal et al., 2023b; El Faydy et al., 2019; Abdulridha et al., 2020a). Hermoso-Diaz et al. conducted a study to assess the anti corrosion properties of linoleic acid on carbon steel in sulfuric acid solution. The study likely involved the use of various experimental techniques, such as electrochemical analysis as well as surface analysis, to determine the effectiveness of linoleic acid in inhibiting the corrosion of carbon steel. The investigations may provide the use of linoleic acid as a potential corrosion inhibitor in industrial applications (Boucherit et al., 2019b). M. Raja et al. reported an inhibition study using 2-mercapto-1-methyl imidazole on zinc metal corrosion in 0.5 N sulfuric acid (Abuthahir et al., 2014). The prevailing idea is to identify the multiplicative characteristics of the inhibitor.

The current study utilizes mass defeat method, electrochemical analysis, and AC impedance spectra to study the corrosion inhibition efficiency of 2-[(furan-3ylmethylene)-amino]-benzenethiol multipart in carbon steel immersed in acidic medium. The aim of the current investigation is to find the apparent activation energies of the corrosion process, which will be based on the concentrations of additives used in the process (Bedair et al., 2020). Additionally, the study aims to analyze the correlation between the temperature and the rate of corrosion. The term “apparent activation energy” refers to the amount of energy required to start a chemical reaction or a process. The study involves performing experiments at different temperatures with varying additive concentrations of the inhibitor to determine the effect of these factors on the rate of corrosion. The results of the study will be useful in designing more effective corrosion inhibitors and developing better strategies for preventing corrosion (Hermoso-Diaz et al., 2019).
The action of amino benzenethiol as an inhibitor to resist deterioration in carbon steel due to corrosion in acidic medium has been studied.

The inhibitor was synthesized by adopting the known Schiff base synthetic route. The name and molecular structure of compound (inhibitor) is shown below. The compound (inhibitor) is 2-[(furan-3ylmethylene)-amino]-benzenethiol with molecular formula is C11H9ONS and molecular weight is 203 g/mol. The colour of the compound is brown and its melting point is 80 °C. The 2-FAB compound is soluble in methanol, ethanol, acetone, DMF, DMS and dichloro methane. The yield of 2-FAB compound is 87.09%.
Image 1

A sheet of carbon steel containing 2.0% C, 0.026% S, 0.06% P, 0.4% Mn, and the remaining major element being iron, was chosen for the current research. In studies related to weight loss and surface examination, carbon steel samples were made to undergo an additional process of polishing to achieve a mirror finish, followed by degreasing with trichloroethylene and phenoxy ethanol. Teflon-encapsulated carbon steel rods of 1 cm exposed cross sections were utilized as working electrodes in the potentiodynamic polarization experiments. A mirror finish was achieved by polishing the electrode and degreasing it with trichloroethylene.

Double distilled water is used to set up all solutions. H2SO4 of analytical grade is used in dilute form. Using a minimum amount of ethanol, the inhibitor was dissolved in the stock solution, and the volume was increased with double distilled water to achieve the desired concentration. The desired concentration was obtained by adding the required volume of the inhibitor stock solution with the H2SO4 solution.

The weight loss technique was executed in accordance with the standard protocol. One day of mass loss protocol entails the dipping of carbon steel specimen in acidic medium and in assorted concentrations of amino benzenethiol (50 ppm–250 ppm) (M'hiri et al., 2016; Dehghani et al., 2019). After being held captive for the duration of 24 h, the tester is entirely drenched in water, dried to devoid any covering and is then weighed on a scale. The effectiveness of inhibition is then calculated using equation (1) as given below
(1)

The values of mass defeat for W
1 and Wo in grams in the above equation are determined by the survival and scarcity of inhibitors as shown in Table 1 . An environment was selected based on its inhibition efficiency among several inhibitor combinations (Abd El-Lateef et al., 2015). The rate of corrosion of carbon steel when submerged in an acidic environment, irrespective of the presence or absence of corrosion inhibitors, were determined by measuring the reduction in weight and the results are displayed in Table 2 . With 250 ppm of inhibitor exposed 2-[(furan-3ylmethylene)-amino]-benzenethiol, it gives 95.51 w % of consumption control. Expanding the focus of the inhibitor resulted in a corresponding increase in the effectiveness of rot restraints. The ability of the N and O molecules adjacent to the delocalized pi electrons to donate electrons also contributes to the creation of more effective inhibitor structure.

The analysis of polarization was carried out on 3-terminal cells. The reference terminal was constructed using a lightweight metallic material, with the cathode visible in 1 cm2 region and the remaining area covered with red coating. A saturated electrode of calomel was utilized as the cathode, while a square-shaped foil of platinum was used as the reference electrode. The surrounding environment of the counter electrode resembles as that a running electrode. The activity terminal and platinum cathode had been drenched in ocean water to remove any trace of inhibitor (Merimi et al., 2023). A brackish extension linked the saturated calomel cathode to the experimental reaction. From the plot of E versus log (I), one can determine the consumption potential (Ecorr) and Tafel slopes (ba and bc). The consumption potential represents the electrode potential at which there is no net consumption or production of the electro active species. It can be obtained by finding the intersection of the Tafel lines, which are straight lines obtained from the plot E versus log (I) for the cathodic and anodic branches of the curve (Krid et al., 2018). The Tafel slopes, ba and bc, are the slopes of the cathodic and anodic Tafel lines, respectively. They are related to the reaction mechanism and kinetics of the electrochemical process. ba is related to the rate-limiting step for the reduction reaction, while bc is related to the rate-limiting step for the oxidation reaction. Overall, by analyzing the plot of E versus log (I), one can gain important insights into the electrochemical behavior of the system under study (Chung et al., 2019a; Boukazoula et al., 2022; Rajamohan et al., 2022).

During the erosion restraint process, a protective layer has been confirmed to be formed on the surface of carbon steel through the use of polarization concentration. If a protection layer forms on the surface of carbon steel, its resistance value of straight polarity (LPR) increases, while the current value of corrosion (Icorr) decreases (Deyab et al., 2022; Jafari et al., 2022). Fig. 1 a, b shows the potentiodynamic polarization curves of carbon steel when immersed in an acidic solution, along with the inhibition efficiency (IE) values in the presence and absence of the inhibitor. Table 3 contains the values of erosion boundaries. According to Fig. 1a, the corrosion potential of carbon steel in 1N H2SO4 is recorded as - 457 mV with SCE during immersion. The value of LPR value is recorded as 17.9 Ohm/cm2. The corresponding corrosion current is found to be 1.593 × 10−3A/cm2. It can be inferred from the above that the protective coating conforms to the cathodic areas on the carbon steel surface. The disintegration of carbon steel is controlled by this layer, which forms a complex of carbon steel and amino benzothiol on the cathodic sites of the carbon steel surface, thereby regulating its cathodic response. Adding 250 ppm of 2-FAB to the aforementioned environment results in a shift of the corrosion potential to the noble side, specifically to −460 mV/SCE (Fig. 1b). Due to the development of a protective film on the surface of the carbon steel, the corrosion potential is shifted towards the more noble side. When the anodic and cathodic reactions of carbon steel dissolution are controlled by this film, LPR is found to increase and Icorr decreases which suggests that the film possesses more corrosion-resistant characteristics (Badiea and AndampMohana, 2009; Alamiery et al., 2021).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of after effects of alternate current impedance
ce:section.ce:para:
The cell plan follows the divergence estimation approach and the framework is allotted around 5–10 min to attain a stable open circuit capacity. Furthermore, an alternating current limit of 10 mV is enforced. The used current frequency was changed from 100 kHz to 100 MHz. The impedance components z' and z'' of the cell are measured in ohms at different frequencies. The following expression is employed to determine the values of the speed of movement resistance and two fold layer capacitance values as mentioned in equ (2).
(2)

The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy, which measures the impedance of alternating current, has been employed to verify the development of a protective film on the surface of carbon steel (Heakal et al., 2011; Esmaeili et al., 2015). Some of the indications of the formation of protective film on the surface of the carbon steel are an increase in the resistance to charge transfer (Rt), the disappearance of double layer capacitance value (Cdl) and an increase in the impedance log (Z/ohm) value. The impedance spectra of carbon steel exposed to an acidic environment, with and without inhibitors, are shown in Fig. 2 (a, b) (Nyquist plots), Fig. 2c (equivalent circuit), and Fig. 3 (a, b) (Bode plots). Table 4 presents the parameters of AC impedance such as charge transfer resistance (Rt) and double layer capacitance (Cdl) obtained from the carbon steel AC impedance spectra when dipped in different solutions. Carbon steel immersed in 1N H2SO4 gives an Rt of 0.02567 Ohm cm2 and Cdl of 7.7933 × 10−4 F cm−2. When 250 ppm of 2-FAB is added to 1 N H2SO4, there is an increase in the Rt value from 0.02567 to 0.51344 Ω cm2 and a decrease in the Cdl value from 7.7933 × 10−4 to 3.896 × 10−5 F cm−2. The value of impedance [log (z/ohm)] is found to increase from 1.012 to 1.122. The above results indicate the formation of protective film on the carbon steel surface (Solomon et al., 2020).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of temperatures of 2-[(furan-3ylmethylene)-amino]-benzenethiol against the corrosion of carbon steel
ce:section.ce:para: The impact of temperature is a significant factor in corrosion research, as it provides insights into the adsorption mechanism of inhibitors. In this instance, the corrosion rate and corrosion efficiency are being evaluated for a 2-FAB inhibitor system within a range of 50–250 ppm, while temperatures is varied between 303 and 333 K. The inhibition efficiency and the corrosion rate for 2-[(furan-3ylmethylene)-amino]-benzenethiol inhibitor are recorded in Table 5 . The inhibition efficiency is found to decrease from 95.51% to 72.60% and the rate of corrosion is observed to rise from 14.27 to 142.67 as the temperature was raised from 303 K to 333 K in 1N H2SO4 at its higher concentration of 250 ppm for 2-[(furan-3ylmethylene)-amino]-benzenethiol inhibitor. An increase in the concentration of the inhibitor leads to a corresponding increase in the impact of temperature on both the dissolution of carbon steel and the partial desorption of inhibitor molecules from its surface (Abdel Hameed et al., 2018). Inhibitor solution concentration has a direct relationship with corrosion inhibition efficiency for different levels of surface coverage at a constant temperature as indicated by the adsorption isotherm. Carbon steel surfaces and inhibitor molecular constituents interact using the adsorption isotherm to provide basic information. Corrosion inhibitor molecules adsorb on carbon steel surface based on several isotherms. A linear relationship can be found between q and inhibitor concentration based on the isotherm. The effectiveness of inhibitors is determined by comparing different isotherms models, which are used in this study (Abdallah et al., 2022).

The weight loss method was used to determine θ, the level of surface coverage for various concentrations of the inhibitor (50–250 ppm at a difference of 50 ppm) and at different temperatures (303 K, 313 K, 323 K, and 333 K). An equation for the surface coverage (q) and concentration of inhibitor (C) can be derived using Langmuir isotherms.

In the above equation the gas constant is represented by R, T indicates the temperature and concentration of CH2O is taken as 55.55 mol. dm−3. Fig. 4 (a–d) illustrate the Langmuir adsorption isotherm plots displaying the relationship between the concentration of adsorbate (C) and the ratio of concentration of adsorbate to the surface coverage (C/θ) at different temperatures for 2-FAB inhibitor. The adsorption parameters received from the Langmuir adsorption isotherm were enlisted in Table 6 for the 2-FAB inhibitor system. The parameters include the values of ΔGo ads, R2, slope, and intercept.

ΔGo ads gives negative values which indicates the spontaneous nature of adsorption and supports the stability of the layer on the carbon steel surface. The value of ΔGo ads can be calculated using the following expression

For the ideal Langmuir adsorption isotherm it is assumed that there is no interaction between the adsorbed inhibitor molecule and the carbon steel surface. The slopes of such a plot should be unity (Negm et al., 2012). But since the slope values deviate from unity it is established that there is definitely an interaction between molecules of the adsorbed inhibitor and the surface of the carbon steel in the current work. There is a mutual attraction or repulsion present between the molecules of the inhibitor that are adsorbed on the anode and the cathode of the carbon steel surface. According to the comparison of R2 values from Langmuir adsorption isotherm, it has been found by researchers that the Langmuir adsorption isotherm provides a better fit at 303 K as compared to higher temperatures (Saxena et al., 2018; Abdulridha et al., 2020b). There is a positive R2 value over 0.9, indicating linearity in Langmuir plots. When the temperature is increased, the Kads value decreases for the inhibitor (2-FAB) on the surface of the carbon steel, indicating strong adsorption at low temperatures (Laadam et al., 2023; Belghiti et al., 2020).

The assumption underlying the Temkin adsorption isotherm is that the 2-FAB inhibitor (monolayer) is evenly distributed on the carbon steel surface. The decrease in adsorption energy is directly proportional to the increase in the values of surface coverage θ. The Temkin adsorption isotherm provided below was used to fit the surface coverage values got from weight loss analysis at different concentrations of 2-FAB in 1N H2SO4 solution.

The process of adsorption of organic molecules at the interface between carbon steel and a solution can be described as a quasi phenomenon of substitution wherein the molecule of the inhibitor replaces water molecule on the carbon steel surface (Pawar et al., 2022).

Where the size parameter is X. The parameter denoted by X denotes the quantity of the H2O molecules adsorbed to that of the inhibitor molecule that is displaced. Fig. 5 (a–d) display the relationship between the angle of polarization (θ) and the logarithm of the concentration of carbon steel in 1N H2SO4 under two conditions: with and without the inhibitor system 2-FAB. These figures show the plot at various temperatures.

Table 7 summarizes the intercept values, slope, a, Kads and ΔGo ads which are determined from Temkin isotherm plots. The negative value of ‘a' for the inhibitor system 2-FAB, at various temperatures indicates the repulsion within the molecules of the adsorbed layer on the carbon steel surface. This observation aligns with the data that has been previously reported (Chung et al., 2019b). The R2 values for the inhibitor system indicate a weak relationship between θ and log C, as they are slightly less than 0.9, approaching unity at different temperatures. The Langmuir isotherm appears to fit better than the Temkin for the chosen set of evaluated green inhibitors, as evidenced by the comparison of the regression coefficients and R2 values. This suggests that the studied inhibitors adhere more closely to the Langmuir than the Temkin isotherm (Haldhar et al., 2021). The ΔGo ads values were negative indicating the spontaneity of adsorption of the inhibitor molecule on to the surface of the carbon steel. Physisorption is indicated by ΔGo ads of up to −20 kJ mol−1 while chemisorption is indicated by ΔGo ads for more than −40 kJ mol−1. Table 9 displays the high Kads values and low negative ΔGo ads values observed between 303 K and 333 K. It clearly indicates the occurrence of physisorption. The possibility of chemisorption cannot be disregarded due to the intricate nature of formation of the complex during the process of corrosion inhibition (Da. Silva et al., 2021; Kumar et al., 2020).

FT-IR study proves to be a highly effective technique for detecting the molecular absorption bands of functional groups and assessing the orientation of the inhibitor on the samples of carbon steel surface. Numerous studies have indicated that the application of FT-IR analysis can be utilized to forecast the type of bonding that occurs between the inhibitor and the surface of carbon steel. FTIR spectrum of 2-[(furan-3ylmethylene)-amino]-benzenethiol is presented in Fig. 6 a. The frequency of C–H stretching is observed at 3410.13 cm−1 (Rathod et al., 2022) while the CN stretching frequency is detected at 1650.10 cm−1. The frequency of S–H stretching is measured as 2720.16 cm−1, and the peak attributed to C–C is located at 1014.13 cm−1. The C–N frequency is observed at 1490.36 cm−1. Fig. 6b illustrates the presence of a thin protective film on the carbon steel surface that has been dipped in 1N H2SO4 containing 250 ppm of 2-[(furan-3ylmethylene)-amino]-benzenethiol. The C–H bond has shifted from 3410.13 cm−1 to 3417.67 cm−1, indicating that molecular adsorption of a water molecule has occurred (Cherrak et al., 2020; Albo Hay Allah et al., 2022). The S–H group exhibits a frequency shift from 2720.16 cm−1 to 2865.42 cm−1, while the CN group displays a frequency shift from 1650.10 cm−1 to 1579.44 cm−1. Additionally, the C–C frequency undergoes a shift from 1014.13 cm−1 to 1060.64 cm−1. Finally, the C–N group's peak at 1590.02 cm−1 is observed to shift to 1399.08 cm−1. The band at 585.86 cm-1 predominantly arises from the Fe-complex, indicating its significant contribution to the overall spectral pattern. Taken together, these bands serve as clear evidence of complex formation on the carbon steel surface.

ce:section.ce:section-title: SEM analysis of the carbon steel surface
ce:section.ce:para: Scanning electron microscopy can generate visual images of carbon steel surface. In order to comprehend the character of the film and the occurrence of 2-FAB inhibitor on the surface of the carbon steel, as well as to investigate the degree of corrosion in carbon steel, a SEM was employed to scrutinize the surface morphology (Mohan et al., 2023). Fig. 7 (a, b, and c) depict SEM micro images of a carbon steel specimen that was submerged in 1N H2SO4 for 2 hrs. The images show the specimen in absence/presence of 2-FAB system. The micrographs obtained from the polished carbon steel surface (control) shows a smooth surface as seen in Fig. 7a. This shows the nonappearance of any other corrosion products, rough surface or any complex compound of the inhibitor formed on the surface of carbon steel (Panneerselvam. et al., 2022). The image of carbon steel surface dipped in 1N H2SO4 (Fig. 7b) show a roughness in the surface of the carbon steel which indicates that the carbon steel immersed in 1N H2SO4 medium is highly corroded. On the other hand, Fig. 7c indicates a suppression in the rate of corrosion in the presence of inhibitor (250 ppm of 2-FAB) which can be directly seen from the decrease in the area of corrosion (Shahabi et al., 2019). The formation of insoluble complex on the surface of the carbon steel leads to the suppression of corrosion. The carbon steel surface shows smoothness almost equal to the polished carbon steel (Aquino-Torres et al., 2020). Thus it is confirmed that the presence of 250 ppm of 2-FAB, makes the surface to be covered by a thin layer of inhibitor to rein the dissolution of carbon steel in 1N H2SO4. In conclusion it is discovered that the efficiency of adsorption at the carbon steel/solution interface is increased in the presence of inhibitor and thus the inhibitors likely leads to the reduction of metallic surfaced structure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: AFM analysis of the carbon steel surface
ce:section.ce:para: The geography of the surfaces kept in 2D, 2D cross sectional profile charts, and 3D pictures was analyzed, and the typical unpleasantness (Ra), surface harshness (RMS), and greatest top-to-valley distance were not set in stone from the separate pictures. In the AFM strategy, the upsides of Sp, Sq, Sa, and Sy for the cleaned carbon steel combination surface (reference) are 718.93, 945.78, 3729.20 and 2456.6 nm respectively, which show a homogeneous surface contrasted with the clear (1986, 458.63, 955.34, and 378) nm, separately (Mohan et al., 2021). This will show that the clear surface has a greater unpleasantness than the cleaned carbon steel surface (Fig. 8 a). Besides, this illustrates that the unprotected surface of carbon steel is more unpleasant because of the erosion of metal in a fluid arrangement of acidic medium (Fig. 8b) (El Bakri et al., 2019). Table 8 shows when consumption inhibitors containing acidic media are present, the normal unpleasantness (Sa) value decreases from 608.25 nm with the inhibitor to 997.91 nm with the clear nature (Tecuapa-Flores et al., 2019). The value shows that the enormous distinction in these boundaries affirms that the surface seems smoother, because of the development of a minimized defensive film which is shown in Fig. 8c [58]. AFM analysis was done to analyze the line roughness, average surface roughness, Ra, RMS value surface roughness, average of the measured height deviations Rq, the maximum peak-to-valley Ry and maximum peak height Rp. Table 9 gives the calculated AFM parameters.

In the current work, amino benzene thiol has been utilized as a consumption inhibitor to decrease the rate of erosion of carbon steel submerged in 1N H2SO4. The unthinking parts of consumption restraint have been proposed in view of the outcomes obtained from the mass misfortune strategy, polarization study, AC impedance estimations, and surface assessment methods like FTIR spectroscopy, Scanning electron microscopy and Atomic force microscopy. The pace of erosion is diminished with expanding increments of natural builds (amino benzene thiol corrosive), likely because of the ever-evolving adsorption of the amino benzene thiol on the carbon steel surface. The weight reduction strategy shows a restraint effectiveness of 95.51%. The polarization concentrate demonstrates that the viable amino benzene thiol corrosive framework act as cathodic inhibitors, controlling the cathodic response. The electrochemical impedance study estimates an expansion in the charge move obstruction (Rt), a diminishing two fold layer capacitance (Cdl), and an expansion in the erosion current (Icorr) values inferable from the expanded thickness of the adsorbed layer. Addition of 250 ppm of 2-FAB to 1N H2SO4, produces an increase in the Rt value from 0.02567 to 0.51344 Ω cm2 and a decrease in the Cdl value from 7.7933 × 10−4 to 3.896 × 10−5 F cm−2. The value of impedance [log (z/ohm)] is also found to increase from 1.012 to 1.122. The values obtained from weight loss method for the studied inhibitor match up properly in the isotherm of Temkin's adsorption. FT-IR spectra uncover that the defensive film comprises the carbon steel-amino benzene thiol complex. The SEM micrographs show the perfection of the carbon steel surface, as cleaned carbon steel. The AFM substantiates the reduction of harshness and pitting of the carbon steel surface after the addition of 2-FAB. The eventual outcome of the review could track down application in pickling industry.

Anwar Sathiq contributed to review and editing. S.S.Syed Abuthahir contributed to validation, writing of the original draft, review and editing. K.S.Mohan contributed to writing of the original draft, validation, investigation, review and editing. S. Berbeth Mary contributed to validation, investigation and methodology. K.V. Gunavathy involved in the characterization analysis of the study.
